sections.title: Global Equities
sections.content: Sharp regional dispersion defined the week as US mega-cap technology drove Wall Street higher while the collapse of the US-Iran ceasefire and a near-**5%** rise in oil weighed on energy-importing markets. MSCI World was roughly flat to slightly higher at about **+0.3%** (approx., verify before merging), MSCI Developed Markets around **+0.3%** (approx., verify before merging) and MSCI Emerging Markets near **-0.4%** (approx., verify before merging). The S&P 500 rose **1.3%** to **7,575**, with the MAG7 cohort leading as Meta gained nearly **15%** and Nvidia climbed about **4%** on Friday. Europe's Euro Stoxx 50 fell roughly **1.8%** (approx., verify before merging) alongside a **1.79%** drop in the STOXX 600, China's CSI 300 slipped (Friday **-1.96%**), Hong Kong's Hang Seng was firmer (Friday **+1.4%**) and Japan's Nikkei 225 lost **1.70%** to about **68,558**.

sections.title: US Equities
sections.content:
US equities led global markets in a technology-driven week, shrugging off renewed Middle East tensions. The S&P 500 rose about **1.3%** to **7,575**, the Nasdaq Composite added roughly **1%** to **26,282** and the Dow Jones Industrial Average climbed to **52,637**, leaving the S&P around **0.5%** below its early-June record.

Mega-cap technology led the charge. Meta jumped nearly **15%**, its best week since early 2024, and Nvidia rose about **4%** on Friday, while SK Hynix's blockbuster Nasdaq debut — pricing at **$149** and opening near **$170** to raise **$26.5bn** — underscored buoyant AI-chip demand. Energy also outperformed on the near-**5%** rise in oil.

Macro signals turned more hawkish. Minutes from the Fed's June meeting showed a few policymakers saw a case for higher rates, with officials divided on the path ahead. ISM services eased to **54.0** from **54.5** and existing home sales fell **2.4%**, as investors looked ahead to June CPI and the start of bank earnings.

sections.title: European Equities
sections.content:
European equities underperformed sharply as the collapse of the US-Iran ceasefire and the two nations' exchange of strikes revived geopolitical risk and pushed oil higher. The pan-European STOXX Europe 600 fell **1.79%** in local-currency terms, with losses broad across the region.

Germany's export-heavy DAX led the decline, down **2.76%**, followed by France's CAC 40 at **-1.99%**, the UK's FTSE 100 at **-1.70%** and Italy's FTSE MIB at **-0.39%**. Higher energy costs and a risk-off tone weighed on cyclicals, though the Euro Stoxx 50 clawed back ground on Friday, rising **1.3%** as technology and mining shares rebounded.

The rates backdrop shifted hawkish. With higher oil rekindling inflation concerns, markets increased expectations for further ECB tightening, adding pressure to rate-sensitive sectors and reinforcing the week's defensive rotation.

sections.title: APAC Equities
sections.content:
Asian markets were mixed as higher oil and renewed geopolitical tension hit energy importers. Japan's Nikkei 225 fell **1.70%** and the TOPIX slipped **0.70%**, pressured by the country's reliance on imported energy and profit-taking in technology after recent strong gains, though the Nikkei rebounded around **1.2%** on Friday to about **68,558**.

Chinese equities diverged. The mainland CSI 300 ended lower (Friday **-1.96%**), dragged by technology and industrials, while Hong Kong's Hang Seng proved firmer, rising **1.4%** on Friday as chipmakers tracked Wall Street higher.

China's data underscored uneven momentum: June CPI rose just **1.0%** year over year, slowing from May, while producer prices climbed **4.1%** — the fastest since July 2022 — on stronger upstream metals, petroleum and coal prices, hinting at firmer industrial pricing even as consumer demand stays subdued.

sections.title: Foreign Exchange
sections.content:
Currency markets were shaped by the interplay of renewed US-Iran tensions, swinging oil and a softer US dollar in the wake of June's weak payrolls. As ships kept moving through the Strait of Hormuz and energy infrastructure stayed intact, risk-off positioning was pared and the dollar traded on the back foot.

The euro and pound posted modest gains against the dollar, reflecting hawkish repricing of ECB policy as higher oil lifted inflation expectations, with sterling holding firm among the majors.

The yen stayed weak, with USD/JPY around **1%** below its 1 July peak of **162.84** and near four-decade lows, keeping intervention risk elevated as JGB yields hovered at multi-decade highs. The yuan remained resilient, with USD/CNH trading near a three-year low.

sections.title: Commodities
sections.content:
Oil reclaimed its role as the dominant cross-asset driver. Crude jumped almost **5%** on the week as the US-Iran ceasefire collapsed and the two sides exchanged strikes, with WTI near **$74** and Brent around **$79**; a Russian ban on diesel exports squeezed an already tight refined-products market and drove fuel prices higher still.

Precious and industrial metals sold off as crude's resurgence lifted yields and revived inflation and rate concerns. Gold slipped below **$4,100** and silver back under **$60**, while the GDX gold-miner ETF fell **2.9%** and copper also weakened.

Fed minutes showing support among some members for rate hikes added to hawkish front-end repricing, reinforcing pressure on non-yielding assets. The higher-oil, higher-yield mix tightened financial conditions and sharpened the divergence between resilient energy and softer metals.

sections.title: Fixed Income
sections.content:
Sovereign yields rose as higher oil and hawkish Fed minutes drove a repricing toward tighter policy. The 10-year UST yield climbed to about **4.56%** and the 2-year to roughly **4.21%**, lifting the front end as markets trimmed easing expectations.

Minutes from the Fed's June meeting revealed a few officials saw a case for raising rates, with the committee divided on the path through year-end. Renewed inflation risk from energy reinforced the higher-for-longer narrative across the curve.

Credit tone stayed broadly steady amid the equity rally, with IG resilient and HY supported by risk appetite, even as rising rates and geopolitical uncertainty argued for selectivity. Edward Jones continued to emphasise quality and diversification, flagging bank earnings and June CPI as the next catalysts.
